quiz.title: Unit 7 Quiz 2, Grade 11 Biology
quiz.subtitle: Cell Division: Interphase, Mitosis, and Meiosis
quiz.subject: Biology
quiz.topic: Sister Chromatids, Mitosis Stages, Interphase Phases, Meiosis I vs Mitosis, Crossing Over, Cell Cycle Checkpoints

questions.question: Which of the following statements about sister chromatids is correct? (Select all that apply)
options: They are identical copies of DNA joined at the centromere.
They separate during anaphase of mitosis.
They exist after DNA replication in the S phase.
They are held together by spindle fibers throughout the entire cell cycle.
Each sister chromatid is considered a separate chromosome before separation.
They are only present in cells undergoing meiosis.
questions.explanation: Sister chromatids are identical DNA copies joined at the centromere (A), formed during S phase (C), and separate during anaphase (B). They are NOT held by spindle fibers throughout the cycle (cohesin holds them), are NOT separate chromosomes until anaphase, and are present in both mitosis and meiosis.

questions.question: In which stage of mitosis do sister chromatids separate and move toward opposite poles?
questions.explanation: Anaphase is defined by the separation of sister chromatids via cleavage of cohesin complexes. They are pulled by spindle fibers to opposite poles of the cell.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between mitosis and meiosis I?
options: Mitosis produces haploid cells; meiosis I produces diploid cells
Sister chromatids separate in mitosis; homologous chromosomes separate in meiosis I
DNA replication occurs only in mitosis
Crossing over occurs only in mitosis
questions.explanation: In mitosis, sister chromatids separate. In meiosis I, homologous chromosomes separate (reducing chromosome number by half). Meiosis II then separates sister chromatids, similar to mitosis.

questions.question: During which meiotic stage does crossing over occur?
questions.explanation: Crossing over (genetic recombination) occurs during pachytene of prophase I, mediated by the synaptonemal complex. This exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes creates genetic diversity.

questions.question: Which of the following is a primary function of mitosis?
options: Repairing damaged tissues and replacing worn-out cells
Halving the chromosome number in cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis functions in growth, tissue repair, and asexual reproduction. It produces identical diploid cells. Gamete production (A), genetic diversity (C), and chromosome halving (D) are functions of meiosis.

questions.question: Which statement best describes the genetic outcome of mitosis?
options: Daughter cells are genetically identical to each other and the parent cell
Daughter cells have half the chromosome number of the parent cell
Daughter cells are genetically unique due to recombination
Mitosis introduces mutations in each cell
questions.explanation: Mitosis produces daughter cells that are genetically identical to each other and to the parent cell. This is essential for growth and repair.

questions.question: Which of the following processes is NOT a function of mitosis?
options: Formation of sperm and egg cells
questions.explanation: Formation of sperm and egg cells (gametes) occurs through meiosis, not mitosis. Mitosis is for growth, repair, and asexual reproduction.

questions.question: What is the main activity of a cell during interphase?
questions.explanation: Interphase is the period of growth and DNA replication. It includes G1 (growth), S (DNA synthesis), and G2 (preparation for division).

questions.question: During which interphase stage does the cell primarily grow and carry out normal metabolic activities?
questions.explanation: G1 phase (Gap 1) is the primary growth phase where the cell increases in size, produces proteins, and performs normal metabolic functions.

questions.question: What occurs during the G2 phase of interphase?
options: Cell growth and preparation for mitosis
questions.explanation: G2 phase involves continued growth, organelle replication, and preparation for mitosis, including synthesis of proteins needed for division.

questions.question: Which of the following is true about interphase?
options: It is a very short phase of the cell cycle
DNA is uncoiled and exists as chromatin
Sister chromatids separate during this phase
questions.explanation: During interphase, DNA is uncoiled and exists as chromatin, allowing for transcription and replication. Interphase is actually the longest phase of the cell cycle.

questions.question: Which structure is duplicated during interphase to ensure proper mitotic spindle formation?
questions.explanation: Centrioles duplicate during interphase (specifically in S phase) to form the two centrosomes that will organize the spindle fibers during mitosis.

questions.question: Which phase of interphase follows DNA replication?
questions.explanation: DNA replication occurs in S phase. After S phase, the cell enters G2 phase, where it continues to grow and prepares for mitosis.

questions.question: How is interphase different from mitosis?
options: DNA replication occurs only during mitosis
Interphase is a period of growth and preparation, not division
questions.explanation: Interphase is the growth and preparation period (G1, S, G2) where the cell is not dividing. Mitosis is the division of the nucleus, followed by cytokinesis.

questions.question: During which phase of interphase does the cell check for DNA errors before mitosis?
questions.explanation: The G2 checkpoint ensures DNA replication is complete and any DNA damage is repaired before the cell enters mitosis. This prevents propagation of mutations.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered the details of interphase and cell division! 🧬
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the differences between mitosis and meiosis I.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on interphase phases and what happens in each.
scoreMessages.0: Please review sister chromatids, interphase activities, and mitotic stages.
